timeline.event: Born in Clausthal, Kingdom of Hanover
Graduated in medicine from the University of Göttingen
Presented the results of anthrax research at the University of Breslau
Clearly identified the anthrax bacillus as the specific causative agent of anthrax
Moved to the Imperial Health Office in Berlin
Discovered the tuberculosis bacillus for the first time
Discovered the cholera pathogen while traveling through Egypt and India
Clearly identified the cholera bacillus as the specific causative agent of cholera
Discovered tuberculin, an antigen against the tuberculosis bacillus
Became the first director of the Institute for Infectious Diseases in Berlin
Awarded the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine for founding bacteriology and discovering the tuberculosis bacillus
Died in Baden-Baden

keyAchievements.title: Identified the causes of anthrax and cholera
keyAchievements.description: Clearly identified pathogenic bacteria as the specific causative agents of anthrax in 1877 and cholera in 1885, earning the evaluation as the 'father of bacteriology'.

keyAchievements.title: Discovered the tuberculosis bacillus
keyAchievements.description: Discovered the tuberculosis bacillus for the first time in 1882 and revealed that it is transmitted through the air.

keyAchievements.title: Founded bacteriological research methodology
keyAchievements.description: Contributed to the development of microbiology by pioneering methods for fixing, staining, and photographing bacteria under a microscope.

keyAchievements.title: Discovered tuberculin
keyAchievements.description: Discovered tuberculin, an antigen against the tuberculosis bacillus, in 1890.

faq.question: Who was Robert Koch?
faq.answer: Robert Koch was a German physician and microbiologist born in 1843 and died in 1910, known as the 'father of bacteriology'.

faq.question: What are Robert Koch's major achievements?
faq.answer: He identified the causative bacteria of anthrax in 1877 and cholera in 1885, and discovered the tuberculosis bacillus for the first time in 1882.

faq.question: When did Robert Koch win the Nobel Prize?
faq.answer: He was awarded the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 1905 for his contributions to founding bacteriology and discovering the tuberculosis bacillus.

faq.question: Did Robert Koch discover tuberculin?
faq.answer: Yes, he discovered tuberculin, an antigen against the tuberculosis bacillus, in 1890, but his reputation temporarily declined when it was later found to be ineffective as a cure.

timeline.event: Born in Tus, Khorasan.
Abandoned his prestigious positions due to religious anguish and other causes, and traveled to Mecca and various regions as a Sufi ascetic.
Retired to his hometown and passed away.

keyAchievements.title: Authored 'The Incoherence of the Philosophers'
keyAchievements.description: Wrote a book criticizing some philosophers for excessively transplanting Greek philosophy into Islam, holding thoughts that contradict the Quran.

keyAchievements.title: Authored 'The Revival of the Religious Sciences' and 'Deliverance from Error'
keyAchievements.description: Left behind masterworks such as 'The Revival of the Religious Sciences' and 'Deliverance from Error' which had a great influence on future generations.

keyAchievements.title: Harmonization of orthodox theology and Sufism
keyAchievements.description: Made his greatest contribution by harmonizing orthodox theology and Sufism, breathing new life into the Islamic religious system.

keyAchievements.title: Defended orthodox Islam
keyAchievements.description: Contributed to adhering to orthodox Islam by criticizing Ibn Sina's philosophy.

keyAchievements.title: Provided a turning point for Islamic scholarship
keyAchievements.description: Brought a turning point to Islamic religious thought by breaking away Islamic scholarship from scholastic theology and reviving the Quran and Hadith.

keyAchievements.title: Established the status of Sufism
keyAchievements.description: Established the status of Sufism within Islam, making the masses of Muslims accept Sufism.

faq.question: How is Al-Ghazali evaluated?
faq.answer: He is referred to as the greatest Muslim after Muhammad, and is a significant figure in the fields of theology, philosophy, and Sufism.

faq.question: What are Al-Ghazali's major works?
faq.answer: His major works include 'The Incoherence of the Philosophers', 'The Revival of the Religious Sciences', and 'Deliverance from Error'.

faq.question: What is Al-Ghazali's greatest contribution?
faq.answer: His greatest contribution is the harmonization of orthodox theology and Sufism, and the defense of orthodox Islam by criticizing the philosophy of Ibn Sina.

faq.question: What turning point did Al-Ghazali bring to Islamic religious thought?
faq.answer: He broke Islamic scholarship away from scholastic theology, reintroduced esoteric elements, established the status of Sufism, and made philosophy more accessible to ordinary Muslims.

timeline.event: Birth
Secluded himself in the village of Qal'at Ibn Salama in the Oran region of Algeria and devoted himself to academic research

Moved to Cairo, Egypt, and engaged in active lecturing, gained the trust of the Mamluk Sultan Barquq, served as a professor in several academic institutions, and was appointed Chief Justice of the Maliki school
Death

keyAchievements.title: Kitab al-'Ibar (Book of Lessons)
keyAchievements.description: A world history that attempts to systematize the Islamic history of West Asia, containing records and examples of the beginnings and progress of Arabs, Persians, Berbers, and other eras.

keyAchievements.title: Muqaddimah (Prolegomena)
keyAchievements.description: An introduction to history included at the beginning of 'Kitab al-'Ibar', considered a world-class masterpiece that examines the circumstances and laws of societal formation and change, and seeks to answer fundamental questions of cultural history.

faq.question: Who is Ibn Khaldun?
faq.answer: Ibn Khaldun is a representative historian, thinker, and politician of the medieval Islamic world who was active from 1332 to 1406. He comes from the prominent Khaldun family, which exiled from Seville, Spain to Tunis.

faq.question: What are Ibn Khaldun's major works?
faq.answer: His major works are the world history 'Kitab al-'Ibar', which attempts to systematize West Asian Islamic history, and its introduction, 'Muqaddimah'. The 'Muqaddimah' is especially evaluated as a world-class masterpiece that examines the laws of societal formation and change.

faq.question: What was Ibn Khaldun's political career like?
faq.answer: He served as a secretary, diplomatic envoy, and chief justice in various Islamic regimes, including the Hafsid, Marinid, and Nasrid dynasties. However, his life was a series of wanderings due to involvement in conspiracies or being ousted by friction. These political experiences later had a huge impact on his ideological system as a scholar.

timeline.event: Born in eastern Iran or Central Asia (approximate date of existence).
Reportedly received his first divine revelation at the age of 30, seeing the supreme being Ahura Mazda.
Faced significant opposition from local priests, wandering for years to preach his monotheistic vision.
Converted King Vishtaspa, securing political support and ensuring the religion's survival.
Died, reportedly assassinated by a rival priest while praying at an altar.
Born in Alcala de Henares, a university town in Madrid
Followed an Italian cardinal to Rome, became a soldier, and participated in the Battle of Lepanto, where he suffered an injury that left his left hand crippled
Captured by pirates and lived as a slave in Algiers for 5 years
Paid his ransom with the help of the Trinitarian Order, returned to Madrid, and began to write
Published the novel 'La Galatea', but it did not gain popularity
Published Part 1 of 'Don Quixote', which gained immense popularity
Completed Part 2 of 'Don Quixote'
Passed away at the age of 69

keyAchievements.title: Don Quixote
keyAchievements.description: A work evaluated by many as the first modern novel in Europe.

keyAchievements.title: La Galatea
keyAchievements.description: Published the novel 'La Galatea', but it did not attract much popularity.

faq.question: What is Miguel de Cervantes's nickname?
faq.answer: He has the nickname 'Prince of Wits' (el Príncipe de los Ingenios).

faq.question: Where was Miguel de Cervantes born?
faq.answer: He was born in Alcala de Henares, a university town in Madrid.

faq.question: What were Miguel de Cervantes's main professions?
faq.answer: He was a novelist, poet, and playwright.

faq.question: How are Miguel de Cervantes's works evaluated?
faq.answer: His works are mentioned among the most important masterpieces in all of literature, and 'Don Quixote' in particular is evaluated as the first modern novel in Europe.

timeline.event: Born
Created the masterpieces 'Inochi futatsu no naka ni ikitaru sakura kana' and 'Kumo oriori hito o yasumuru tsukimi kana'
Created the masterpiece 'Furuike ya kawazu tobikomu mizu no oto' (Old pond / a frog jumps in / sound of water)
Died (November 28, lunar October 12)

keyAchievements.title: Recognition of Haikai-no-renga style
keyAchievements.description: His works were recognized during his lifetime in the collaborative haikai-no-renga style.

keyAchievements.title: Master of Haiku
keyAchievements.description: Today, he is considered a master of haiku, which was called hokku during his time.

keyAchievements.title: Internationally renowned poet
keyAchievements.description: His poetry is internationally renowned, and many of his poems in Japan are reproduced at monuments or historical sites.

keyAchievements.title: Fame across Japan
keyAchievements.description: He entered the world of poetry at a young age, and by integrating himself into the intellectual landscape of Edo, he became famous throughout Japan.

keyAchievements.title: Inspiration through travel
keyAchievements.description: He wandered throughout Japan, including the north, east, and west, to gain inspiration for his works.

keyAchievements.title: Poetry based on experiences of nature
keyAchievements.description: His poetry was influenced by his direct experiences of the world around him, condensing emotions about landscapes into simple elements.

faq.question: Who was Matsuo Basho?
faq.answer: Matsuo Basho, born in 1644 and died on November 28, 1694 (lunar October 12), was a poet of the early Edo period. His childhood name was Kinsaku, and he was also known as Matsuo Chuemon Munefusa.

faq.question: What are Matsuo Basho's major achievements?
faq.answer: During his lifetime, his works were recognized in the collaborative haikai-no-renga style, and today he is considered a master of haiku. His poetry is internationally renowned and reproduced at monuments and historical sites across Japan.

faq.question: What are Matsuo Basho's representative works?
faq.answer: Representative works include 'Inochi futatsu no naka ni ikitaru sakura kana' and 'Kumo oriori hito o yasumuru tsukimi kana' from 1685, and 'Furuike ya kawazu tobikomu mizu no oto' from 1686.

faq.question: How did Matsuo Basho conduct his literary activities?
faq.answer: While making a living as a teacher, he distanced himself from the social and urban life where literary circles were based, wandering throughout Japan to gain inspiration. His poetry was influenced by direct experiences of the surrounding world, condensing his emotions about landscapes.

faq.question: Which of his works did Matsuo Basho consider most important?
faq.answer: He believed his best work lay in participating in and leading renku, and considered the connection with haikai verses more important than hokku.

timeline.event: Born to Pepin the Short and Bertrada of Laon in Herstal, Wallonia, Frankish Kingdom, near Liege, Belgium.
His father, Pepin the Short, became King of the Franks with the approval of Pope Zachary.
Pope Stephen II bestowed the title of King on Pepin and his sons Charles and Carloman.
Succeeded to the throne after the death of his father Pepin the Short, taking over Austrasia and the northern regions.
Became sole ruler after the death of his brother Carloman I.
Began attacking the Saxons.
Destroyed and annexed the Lombard Kingdom in northern Italy.
Attended the Easter festival in Rome with the Pope and reaffirmed the promise to transfer the Papal States.
Suffered a crushing defeat by the Basques at the Battle of Roncevaux Pass during an expedition to the Iberian Peninsula.
Visited Rome, had his two sons Pepin and Louis consecrated, and bestowed upon them the crowns of Lombardy and Aquitaine respectively.
His wife Hildegard and mother Bertrada of Laon passed away successively, and he remarried Fastrada.
Conquered Thuringia and Bavaria, and deposed Duke Tassilo III of Bavaria.
Soundly defeated the Avars along the Danube River and drove them beyond the borders.
Built a palace and royal church in Aachen, northwestern Germany, making it the de facto capital.
Pope Leo III, attacked by opponents, fled to Charlemagne's court to seek assistance.
Was crowned Emperor of the Romans by Pope Leo III in St. Peter's Basilica.
Louis the Pious captured Barcelona.
The Avar Khagan Zodan visited Aachen, swore fealty to Charlemagne, and converted to Christianity.
Croatian chiefs, including Viseslav, voluntarily surrendered.
Eastern Roman Emperor Michael I Rangabe recognized Charlemagne as Emperor.
Declared his surviving son Louis I the Pious as the legitimate successor at the court in Aachen and crowned him co-emperor.
Died of complications from pleurisy and depression at the Palace of Aachen.
Pope Paschal III canonized Charlemagne as a saint at the request of Frederick I.

keyAchievements.title: Expansion of the Frankish Kingdom
keyAchievements.description: Greatly expanded the Frankish Kingdom by conquering most of western and central Europe, achieving the political unification of Western Europe.

keyAchievements.title: Crowned Emperor of the Romans
keyAchievements.description: Received the title of Emperor of the Romans from Pope Leo III in December 800, politically and religiously unifying most of Western Europe for the first time since the Roman Empire.

keyAchievements.title: Carolingian Renaissance
keyAchievements.description: Initiated the Carolingian Renaissance by greatly developing arts, religion, and culture through the church during his reign.

keyAchievements.title: Codification of laws and centralization
keyAchievements.description: Codified the laws of various tribes to rule over vast territories and enabled centralized rule by dispatching officials from the center.

keyAchievements.title: Spread and integration of Christianity
keyAchievements.description: Sought ideological unity by converting conquered tribes to Catholicism and supported proselytization by dispatching missionaries.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of cultural and educational institutions
keyAchievements.description: Led a revival of learning and arts by building a palace and royal church in Aachen, and founding a royal library and palace school.

faq.question: When was Charlemagne born?
faq.answer: There are theories that Charlemagne was born on April 2 of 740 or 742, or in 747.

faq.question: Why is Charlemagne called the 'Father of Europe'?
faq.answer: He is called the 'Father of Europe' because the Carolingian Renaissance laid the foundation for the current identity of Europe.

faq.question: When did Charlemagne become Emperor of the Romans?
faq.answer: Charlemagne received the title of Emperor of the Romans from Pope Leo III in December 800.

faq.question: How tall was Charlemagne?
faq.answer: According to Einhard's records and the results of a tomb excavation in 1861, Charlemagne was about 190 to 191 cm tall.

faq.question: What languages did Charlemagne speak?
faq.answer: In addition to his mother tongue, he was fluent in Latin and could also speak some Greek.

faq.question: Where was Charlemagne born?
faq.answer: Charlemagne was born in Herstal, Wallonia, Frankish Kingdom, near Liege, Belgium.

timeline.event: Born in Falaise, Duchy of Normandy
Received oaths of fealty as the successor from the Norman magnates
Succeeded to the Duchy of Normandy as his father Robert I died in Nicaea
Started to assert his authority over the duchy by suppressing rebellions
Victorious at the Battle of Val-es-Dunes with the help of King Henry I of France
Succeeded in driving out Guy of Burgundy
Married Matilda of Flanders
King Edward of England seems to have chosen William as his heir
Defended against a two-front attack on Normandy by Henry and Norman rebels
Henry and Geoffrey Martel were defeated at the Battle of Varaville
Firmly secured his hold on the Duchy of Normandy
Seized control of neighboring Maine
Secured dominion over Maine
Invaded England with a massive fleet
Defeated and killed Harold at the Battle of Hastings
Crowned King of England at Westminster Abbey in London
Made preparations for the governance of England and returned to Normandy
Suppressed the rebellion of Edwin and Morcar, and built castles such as Warwick, York, and Nottingham
Suppressed Edgar the Aetheling's rebellion attacking York
Met with three papal legates to hold a council to reform and reorganize the English Church
Suppressed the last northern rebellion and imprisoned Morcar
Attacked Scotland, defeating Malcolm, and signed the Treaty of Abernethy
Ended the war by capturing Le Mans in Maine
Suppressed the 'Revolt of the Earls'
Defeated by King Philip of France at the Battle of Dol
Faced problems with his eldest son, Robert
Unhorsed by Robert during the siege of Gerberoy
Reached a compromise with Robert, reaffirming he would inherit Normandy upon William's death
Visited Wales
Ordered the arrest of his half-brother Odo
Death of his wife, Queen Matilda
Ordered the compilation of the Domesday Book
The Domesday Book work was mostly completed and he received the Oath of Salisbury
Launched an expedition to the Vexin in France
Died at the Priory of St Gervase in Rouen

keyAchievements.title: Solidified position as Duke of Normandy
keyAchievements.description: Firmly secured the Duchy of Normandy as his own around 1060 after a long struggle over the ducal position.

keyAchievements.title: Conquest of England
keyAchievements.description: Led the Norman conquest by invading England in 1066, winning the Battle of Hastings, and being crowned King of England.

keyAchievements.title: Compilation of the Domesday Book
keyAchievements.description: Ordered the compilation of the Domesday Book in 1086, a survey record documenting all land ownership in England.

keyAchievements.title: Laid the foundation for English governance
keyAchievements.description: Constructed various castles in England, settled Norman nobles, and changed the composition of the English clergy.

keyAchievements.title: Alliance with Flanders
keyAchievements.description: Gained a powerful ally in the neighboring County of Flanders through his marriage to Matilda of Flanders.

faq.question: When was William I born?
faq.answer: William I was born on November 8, 1028, in Falaise, Duchy of Normandy.

faq.question: How did William I become the King of England?
faq.answer: Following the death of Edward the Confessor in 1066, he invaded England, won the Battle of Hastings, and was crowned King of England in London on Christmas Day of that year.

faq.question: What is the Domesday Book?
faq.answer: The Domesday Book is a survey record ordered by William I in 1086 that documented the current status of all land ownership in England.

faq.question: When did William I die?
faq.answer: William I died on September 9, 1087, at the Priory of St Gervase in Rouen, while fighting a war in northern France.

faq.question: What was William I's family background like?
faq.answer: He was born as the illegitimate son of Robert I, Duke of Normandy, and his mistress Herleva. He married Matilda of Flanders, with whom he had four sons and either five or six daughters.

timeline.event: Giuseppe Maria Garibaldi, an Italian general and patriot, is born.
Certified as a merchant navy captain.
Met Giovanni Battista Cuneo, an Italian political immigrant, in Taganrog, Russia, and joined the Young Italy movement.
Sentenced to death after participating in the Piedmont rebellion and fled to Marseille.
Joined the Ragamuffin rebels in Brazil and aided the independence of the Riograndense Republic.
Anita Ribeiro joined Garibaldi's ship 'Rio Pardo'.
First son Menotti is born.
Moved to Montevideo and worked as a trader and teacher.
Married Anita in Montevideo; commanded the Uruguayan fleet and raised the 'Italian Legion'.
Second daughter Rosita is born; formed the Italian Legion in Montevideo.
Third daughter Teresita is born.
Achieved victory at the Battles of Cerro and San Antonio.
Returned to his homeland Italy upon hearing news of the revolution breaking out in Palermo.
Promoted to Marshal of the Roman Republic army; fled to northern Italy after the fall of the Roman Republic; his wife Anita died during the retreat.
Became a resident of New York.
Sailed to the mouth of the River Tyne in North East England as the master of the sailing ship Commonwealth.
Returned to Italy.
Outbreak of the Second Italian War of Independence; led the 'Hunters of the Alps' and captured Varese and Como.
Landed in Marsala, Sicily leading a thousand volunteers.
Defeated the Bourbon French garrison at Calatafimi.
Launched a siege on Palermo.
Entered the capital of Naples.
The volunteers achieved success at the Battle of the Volturno.
Met and shook hands with Victor Emmanuel II in Teano.
The unified Kingdom of Italy was established.
Left Genoa, landed in Palermo, and gathered volunteers under the slogan 'Rome or Death'.
Landed at Melito and marched into the Calabrian mountains.
Met regular forces at the Aspromonte mountains and was taken prisoner after a battle.
With the backing of the Italian government, recruited the 'Hunters of the Alps', led them into Trentino, and defeated the Austrian army at Bezzecca.
Marched on Rome again but was stopped by the Papal army supported by French reinforcements.
The Franco-Prussian War broke out.
Died on the island of Caprera.

keyAchievements.title: Contribution to Italian unification
keyAchievements.description: Contributed to the unification of Italy and the establishment of the Kingdom of Italy.

keyAchievements.title: Hero of Two Worlds
keyAchievements.description: Known as the 'Hero of Two Worlds' for his military achievements across South America and Europe.

keyAchievements.title: Support for South American independence
keyAchievements.description: Joined the Ragamuffin rebels in Brazil to help the independence of the Riograndense Republic, and participated in the Uruguayan Civil War, raising the Redshirts.

keyAchievements.title: Italian military command
keyAchievements.description: Appointed general by the provisional government of Milan and promoted to Marshal of the Roman Republic army.

keyAchievements.title: Commanding the Hunters of the Alps
keyAchievements.description: Led the 'Hunters of the Alps' in the Second Italian War of Independence, capturing Varese and Como.

keyAchievements.title: Success of the Expedition of the Thousand
keyAchievements.description: Led an expedition of a thousand volunteers to successfully annex Sicily, Southern Italy, Marche, and Umbria to the Kingdom of Sardinia.

keyAchievements.title: Participation in the Franco-Prussian War
keyAchievements.description: Served as commander of the Army of the Vosges during the Franco-Prussian War.

keyAchievements.title: International symbol
keyAchievements.description: Became an international symbol for realizing the ideals of national independence and republicanism.

keyAchievements.title: Efforts to limit papal power
keyAchievements.description: Contributed to anti-clericalism among Latin liberals and did much to limit the temporal power of the Pope.

faq.question: Who was Giuseppe Garibaldi?
faq.answer: An Italian general and patriot who contributed to the unification of Italy and the establishment of the Kingdom of Italy.

faq.question: Why is Garibaldi called the 'Hero of Two Worlds'?
faq.answer: Because his military achievements span across South America and Europe.

faq.question: What were Garibaldi's political beliefs?
faq.answer: He was a republican who dedicated himself to unifying Italy under a democratic republican government.

faq.question: What is Garibaldi's iconic attire?
faq.answer: It is related to the red shirts worn by his volunteers instead of uniforms.

faq.question: What activities did Garibaldi undertake in South America?
faq.answer: He joined the Ragamuffin rebels in Brazil to aid the independence of the Riograndense Republic, and participated in the Uruguayan Civil War, raising the Redshirts.

faq.question: Did Garibaldi have a chance to participate in the American Civil War?
faq.answer: He was offered a position as a major general in the Union Army by President Abraham Lincoln, but the offer fell through when he proposed the abolition of slavery and the position of Commander-in-Chief as conditions.

faq.question: Where is Garibaldi's hometown?
faq.answer: He was born in Nice (Nizza), currently in France, while it was under French rule in 1792.

faq.question: With whom did Garibaldi have a conflict after Italian unification?
faq.answer: He deeply disliked the Sardinian Prime Minister Camillo di Cavour, owing to personal resentment over Cavour's pragmatism and the selling of Nice to the French.

timeline.event: Born in Edinburgh, Scotland
Mother died (age 8)
Produced his first scientific work, a paper on 'Oval Curves', at age 14
Enrolled at the University of Edinburgh
Began studying at the University of Cambridge
Graduated from Cambridge as Second Wrangler in the Mathematical Tripos and first in the Smith's Prize exam
Elected as a Fellow of Trinity College to supervise experiments on electricity and magnetism and research color theory
Published a paper on the structure of Saturn's rings
Married Katherine Mary Dewar
Awarded the Adams Prize for his research on Saturn's rings
Appointed Professor of Natural Philosophy and Astronomy at King's College London, and received the Rumford Medal from the Royal Society of London for his research on color vision
Produced the first color photograph by applying the mixing of the three primary colors
Published 'A Dynamical Theory of the Electromagnetic Field'
Resigned from his professorship at King's College and became a professor at Cambridge University
Statistically formulated the kinetic theory of gases
Researched at the Cavendish Laboratory
Published 'A Treatise on Electricity and Magnetism'
Began editing Henry Cavendish's unpublished electrical experiment data
Died of stomach cancer in Cambridge at the age of 48 on November 5

keyAchievements.title: Electromagnetism
keyAchievements.description: Laid a unified foundation for electrical and magnetic phenomena, established electromagnetism, and proved that light is an electromagnetic wave.

keyAchievements.title: Maxwell's equations
keyAchievements.description: Mathematically organized existing electromagnetic theories and established Maxwell's equations, the differential equations that form the basis of electromagnetism.

keyAchievements.title: Kinetic theory of gases
keyAchievements.description: Formulated the velocity distribution law of gas molecules and introduced probabilistic concepts, laying the foundation for statistical mechanics.

keyAchievements.title: Color theory and color photography
keyAchievements.description: Produced the first color photograph in 1861 by applying the principle that all colors can be expressed by mixing the three primary colors.

keyAchievements.title: Research on Saturn's rings
keyAchievements.description: Showed that Saturn's rings must be composed of small particles to be stable, a prediction proven by the Voyager spacecraft in the 1980s.

faq.question: Who was James Clerk Maxwell?
faq.answer: A British theoretical physicist and mathematician born in Edinburgh, Scotland, recognized as the founder of electromagnetism for establishing a unified foundation for electrical and magnetic phenomena.

faq.question: What is Maxwell's most important achievement?
faq.answer: Laying a unified foundation for electrical and magnetic phenomena and establishing electromagnetism, as well as presenting 'Maxwell's equations' that mathematically structured this.

faq.question: What contribution did Maxwell make to color photography?
faq.answer: In 1861, he created the first color photograph by applying the principle that all colors can be expressed by mixing the three primary colors.

faq.question: When and where was Maxwell born, and when did he die?
faq.answer: Born on June 13, 1831, in Edinburgh, Scotland, and died of stomach cancer in Cambridge on November 5, 1879, at the age of 48.

timeline.event: Born to King Henry VIII and Anne Boleyn at Greenwich Palace
Mother Anne Boleyn is executed, and she loses her title as princess
Henry VIII dies, and her half-brother Edward VI ascends the throne
Implicated in Thomas Wyatt's rebellion and imprisoned in the Tower of London during the reign of Mary I
Mary I dies, and Elizabeth ascends the throne
Enacts the Act of Supremacy, declaring herself the Supreme Governor of the Church of England
Signs the Treaty of Edinburgh, driving French forces out of Scotland
The Pope excommunicates Elizabeth
Francis Drake completes his circumnavigation of the globe
Executes Mary, Queen of Scots
The English Navy defeats the Spanish Armada
Gives the 'Golden Speech' to Parliament
Passes away at Richmond Palace
메리 1세 사망 후 잉글랜드 및 아일랜드 왕국의 군주로 즉위함
웨스트민스터 사원에서 대관식을 가짐
새로운 수장령이 공포됨
로버트 더들리의 아내 에이미가 사망함
프랑스의 르아브르를 침공함
로버트 더들리가 레스터 백작으로 책봉됨
스코틀랜드의 메리 1세가 단리 경 헨리 스튜어트와 재혼함
스코틀랜드의 메리 1세가 아들 제임스를 낳음
단리 경이 살해됨
스코틀랜드의 메리 1세가 잉글랜드로 망명함
잉글랜드 북부에서 대규모 가톨릭 반란이 일어남
교황 비오 5세가 엘리자베스 1세를 사생아로 선언함
로버트 더들리가 레티스 놀리스와 재혼함
반가톨릭 형벌법이 입법됨
먼스터에서 제럴드 피츠제럴드의 반란이 일어남
험프리 길버트가 북아메리카에 식민지를 세움
잉글랜드-스페인 전쟁이 발발함
프랜시스 드레이크가 카디스를 습격하여 스페인 함대를 대파함
스코틀랜드의 메리 1세가 참수됨
스페인이 무적함대를 결성함
잉글랜드가 스페인에 대규모 원정 함대(드레이크-노리스 원정)를 보냄
프랑스의 앙리 4세 왕위 계승에 원군을 파견함
프랑스에 파견된 잉글랜드 원정군이 궤멸함
휴 오닐이 이끄는 아일랜드 반군과의 전쟁이 시작됨
로버트 데버루가 지휘하는 잉글랜드 군이 아일랜드에서 패배함
잉글랜드 동인도 회사를 인가함
잉글랜드 하원이 가격 담합 폐단을 없애라고 청원함
에식스 백작 로버트 데버루가 처형됨
지인의 죽음으로 건강이 급격히 악화됨
리치몬드궁에서 사망함

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Church of England
keyAchievements.description: Through the Elizabethan Religious Settlement, she firmly established the Church of England as the state religion and sought stability amidst religious conflict.

keyAchievements.title: Defeat of the Spanish Armada
keyAchievements.description: Defeated the Invincible Armada of Spain in 1588, achieving maritime supremacy.

keyAchievements.title: Flourishing of culture and arts
keyAchievements.description: Great playwrights like William Shakespeare and Christopher Marlowe were active during her reign, marking a golden age of English literature.

keyAchievements.title: Support for overseas expansion
keyAchievements.description: Supported overseas exploration, such as Francis Drake's circumnavigation, and laid the foundation for the colonial empire, including establishing the East India Company.

keyAchievements.title: National integration and stability
keyAchievements.description: Maintained domestic political stability and national integration over a 44-year reign, ushering in the 'Elizabethan era'.

keyAchievements.title: 스페인 무적함대 격파
keyAchievements.description: 스페인과의 잉글랜드-스페인 전쟁에서 무적함대가 잉글랜드 해군의 공격에 대패하는 데 기여했습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 잉글랜드 동인도 회사 인가
keyAchievements.description: 1600년 12월 31일 잉글랜드 동인도 회사를 인가하여 해외 식민지 개척의 기반을 마련했습니다.

faq.question: Did Elizabeth I ever marry?
faq.answer: She never married, earning the nickname 'The Virgin Queen'. She utilized her marriage prospects as a diplomatic tool but ultimately famously stated she was married to the nation.

faq.question: Why did she execute Mary, Queen of Scots?
faq.answer: Mary, Queen of Scots, was a Catholic and the next in line to the English throne, becoming a focal point for rebellions and assassination plots against Elizabeth, which led to her eventual execution.

faq.question: What is the relationship between Elizabeth I and the English Renaissance?
faq.answer: Her reign is regarded as the golden age of the English Renaissance, flourishing with culture, arts, and overseas exploration, often called the 'Elizabethan era'.

faq.question: Who succeeded Elizabeth I?
faq.answer: Since she left no heirs, James VI of Scotland (son of Mary, Queen of Scots) succeeded her as James I of England, bringing the Tudor dynasty to an end and starting the Stuart dynasty.

faq.question: 엘리자베스 1세 재위 중 가장 중요한 대외 사건은 무엇인가요?
faq.answer: 스페인과의 잉글랜드-스페인 전쟁에서 스페인의 무적함대를 격파한 사건이 가장 중요하며, 해외 식민지 개척과 잉글랜드 동인도 회사 인가도 이루어졌습니다.

timeline.event: Born into slavery in Holly Springs, Mississippi
Emancipation Proclamation issued, freeing her family
Parents and younger brother die of yellow fever; Ida takes responsibility for her siblings
Forcibly removed from a first-class train car; files a lawsuit against the railroad company
Wins the lawsuit at the local court
The Tennessee Supreme Court overturns the ruling in favor of the railroad company
A friend's lynching incident occurs; she begins a full-scale anti-lynching campaign in 'The Free Speech and Headlight'
Her newspaper office is destroyed by a white mob; she relocates to Chicago
Publishes the anti-lynching pamphlet 'Southern Horrors: Lynch Law in All Its Phases'
Publishes another anti-lynching pamphlet 'The Red Record'
Marries Ferdinand L. Barnett
Participates in the founding of the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP)

keyAchievements.title: Anti-lynching Journalism
keyAchievements.description: Through investigative journalism, she systematically investigated and exposed the reality that the lynching of Black people in the South was often used to eliminate Black people who achieved economic success, rather than to punish criminals.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of Anti-lynching Pamphlets
keyAchievements.description: Published pamphlets containing the realities of lynching, such as 'Southern Horrors' (1892) and 'The Red Record' (1895), drawing national and international attention to the issue.

keyAchievements.title: Founding of Civil Rights Organizations
keyAchievements.description: Participated in the founding of the National Association of Colored Women (NACW) in 1896 and the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP) in 1909.

faq.question: What kind of environment was Ida B. Wells born into?
faq.answer: She was born into slavery in Holly Springs, Mississippi in 1862, but was freed the following year by the Emancipation Proclamation.

faq.question: What motivated Ida B. Wells to start her anti-lynching campaign?
faq.answer: In 1892, a close friend and two other Black men were lynched by a white mob out of economic jealousy over their successful grocery store. This incident prompted her to begin a full-scale anti-lynching campaign.

faq.question: What challenges did Ida B. Wells face because of her anti-lynching campaign?
faq.answer: Her newspaper office in Memphis was destroyed by a white mob, and she received numerous death threats, eventually forcing her to leave the South and relocate to Chicago.

faq.question: What is the Alpha Suffrage Club?
faq.answer: It is the first Black women's suffrage organization in Illinois, founded by Ida B. Wells in Chicago in 1913.

timeline.event: Born in Leiden, Netherlands.
Apprenticed for three years under painter Jacob van Swanenburgh.
Apprenticed for six months under painter Pieter Lastman in Amsterdam.
Opened his own studio in Leiden.
Began accepting students.
Moved to Amsterdam, began working as a professional portrait painter, and married Saskia van Uylenburgh.
Purchased an upscale house in Sint Antoniesbreestraat, Amsterdam.
His wife Saskia passed away shortly after the birth of their son Titus.

keyAchievements.title: Master of Chiaroscuro
keyAchievements.description: Pioneered the technique of chiaroscuro, maximizing the dramatic effect by dramatically contrasting light and dark.

keyAchievements.title: Innovations in Portraiture
keyAchievements.description: Moved away from conventional formal portraits, emphasizing the inner psychology and emotional states of the subjects through his portraits.

keyAchievements.title: Production of Self-Portraits
keyAchievements.description: Left behind numerous self-portraits over his lifetime, documenting the process of his self-reflection and changes in his life.

keyAchievements.title: Excellence in Printmaking
keyAchievements.description: Left a significant mark on the history of printmaking with his outstanding skill in etching, elevating printmaking to an independent art form.

keyAchievements.title: Dutch Golden Age Art
keyAchievements.description: Led the Dutch Golden Age art of the 17th century, creating a wide variety of works encompassing religious themes, history paintings, and landscapes.

faq.question: What is Rembrandt's most famous painting?
faq.answer: His most famous painting is 'The Night Watch' (De Nachtwacht), a group portrait completed in 1642 depicting a militia company moving into action.

faq.question: Why did Rembrandt paint so many self-portraits?
faq.answer: He painted self-portraits as a means to explore his inner self, document his aging process, practice facial expressions and lighting, and sometimes to showcase his skills.

faq.question: What technique is Rembrandt best known for?
faq.answer: He is famous for chiaroscuro, a technique that highlights the subject and creates dramatic tension by strongly contrasting light and shadow.

faq.question: Did Rembrandt enjoy wealth during his lifetime?
faq.answer: He achieved great success and wealth in his early years, but later in life, he suffered from financial difficulties due to lavish spending and changes in artistic trends, eventually declaring bankruptcy.

timeline.event: Born in Portsmouth, England
Father John Dickens is imprisoned for debt; Charles begins working at a blacking factory
Starts career as a journalist
Publishes 'Sketches by Boz'
Gains explosive popularity with the serialization of 'The Pickwick Papers'; marries Catherine Hogarth
Publishes 'Oliver Twist'
First visit to the United States
Publishes 'A Christmas Carol'
Publishes 'David Copperfield'
Purchases Gad's Hill Place, a house he admired since childhood
Separates from his wife Catherine
Publishes 'A Tale of Two Cities'
Publishes 'Great Expectations'
Involved in the Staplehurst rail crash
Second visit to the United States and conducts reading tours
Dies of a stroke; buried in Poet's Corner at Westminster Abbey
《데이비드 코퍼필드》를 출판했다.
《A Child's History of England》를 출판했다.
《황폐한 집》을 출판했다.
《어려운 시절》을 출판했다.
《작은 도릿》을 출판했다.
《두 도시 이야기》를 출판했다.
《위대한 유산》을 출판했다.
《Our Mutual Friend》를 출판했다.
두 번째 미국 방문이 이루어졌다.
《에드윈 드루드의 비밀》을 출판했다.
58세의 나이로 개즈 힐에서 숨을 거두었다.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of memorable characters
keyAchievements.description: Created some of the world's most famous fictional characters, including Ebenezer Scrooge, Oliver Twist, and David Copperfield.

keyAchievements.title: Advocacy for social reform
keyAchievements.description: Strongly criticized the social contradictions, poverty, and child labor issues brought about by the Industrial Revolution through his novels, contributing to social reform.

keyAchievements.title: Popularization of the serialization format
keyAchievements.description: Greatly contributed to the popularization of the novel serialization format by publishing novels in weekly or monthly installments, receiving tremendous response from readers.

keyAchievements.title: Modernization of Christmas
keyAchievements.description: Often referred to as 'The Man Who Invented Christmas,' his work 'A Christmas Carol' played a significant role in establishing the modern culture and concept of Christmas.

faq.question: What is Charles Dickens's most representative work?
faq.answer: His representative works include 'Oliver Twist', 'A Christmas Carol', 'David Copperfield', 'A Tale of Two Cities', and 'Great Expectations'.

faq.question: What was Dickens's childhood like?
faq.answer: His father was imprisoned for debt, forcing him to work in a blacking factory at the age of 12. This harsh experience deeply influenced his later works.

faq.question: Why did Dickens's novels usually come out in serial form?
faq.answer: He published them in weekly or monthly magazines to make his works accessible to the public at an affordable price, and this serialization method became very popular during the Victorian era.

faq.question: Did Dickens's works actually influence society?
faq.answer: Yes, his vivid descriptions of the plight of the poor and the poor conditions of child labor raised public awareness and contributed to real social reforms.

faq.question: How did Dickens die?
faq.answer: He died of a stroke at his home, Gad's Hill Place, on June 9, 1870, at the age of 58.

timeline.event: Born as the son of a vicar in Berkeley, Gloucestershire.
Became an apprentice to surgeon Daniel Ludlow in Sodbury.
Began studying medicine under John Hunter at St George's Hospital, University of London.
Became a general practitioner in his hometown.
Developed an interest in smallpox.
Gathered his observations on smallpox.
Married Catharine Kingscote.
Received his MD from the University of St Andrews in Scotland.
John Hunter died, ending their friendship.
Successfully performed the first vaccination on a local boy.
Reported the results of 23 experiments to the Royal Society and published his paper 'An Inquiry into the Causes and Effects of the Variolae Vaccinae'.
Received a grant from the government (part of a total of £30,000).
The Royal Jennerian Society was founded to promote vaccination in London.
Received the remainder of the grant from the government.
Made his last visit to London.
Died of a stroke in his hometown.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of Cowpox Vaccine
keyAchievements.description: Discovered the cowpox vaccine, earning him the title 'Father of Immunology'.

keyAchievements.title: Coined the Term Vaccine
keyAchievements.description: Coined the terms 'vaccine' and 'vaccination' from the Latin word 'vacca', meaning cow.

keyAchievements.title: Development and Distribution of Smallpox Vaccine
keyAchievements.description: Successfully performed the first vaccination on a boy on May 14, 1796, developing the smallpox vaccine and contributing to its worldwide distribution.

faq.question: Who is Edward Jenner?
faq.answer: Edward Jenner was an English physician, the discoverer of the cowpox vaccine, and the father of immunology.

faq.question: When was Edward Jenner born and when did he die?
faq.answer: Edward Jenner was born on May 17, 1749, and died on January 26, 1823.

faq.question: What led Edward Jenner to coin the term vaccine?
faq.answer: Jenner created the terms 'vaccine' and 'vaccination' from 'vacca', the Latin word for cow.

faq.question: What are the main achievements of Edward Jenner?
faq.answer: Edward Jenner discovered the cowpox vaccine and successfully administered the first vaccination to a boy on May 14, 1796, developing the smallpox vaccine.

timeline.event: Born at the rectory in Steventon, Hampshire.
Studied at the Reading Abbey Girls' School in Reading, Berkshire.
Started writing novels at the age of 14.
Wrote the epistolary novel 'Elinor and Marianne'.
Met a young Irishman named Tom Lefroy and wrote 'First Impressions'.
Began writing 'Sense and Sensibility' based on 'Elinor and Marianne'.
Her father relinquished his rectory and the family moved to Bath, Somerset.
Received a marriage proposal from Harris Bigg-Wither but rejected it the next day.
Sold 'Lady Susan' to Crosby & Co.
Her father died.
Settled in a cottage in Chawton with the help of her third brother, Edward.
Submitted 'Mansfield Park' and published 'Sense and Sensibility' anonymously.
Published 'Pride and Prejudice', based on 'First Impressions'.
Published 'Mansfield Park'.
Dedicated 'Emma' to the Prince Regent (later George IV) shortly before its publication.
Her health frequently deteriorated, leaving her bedridden for long periods.
Died in Winchester at the age of 42 while writing 'Sanditon'.
'Northanger Abbey' and 'Persuasion' were published posthumously.

keyAchievements.title: Began Writing Novels
keyAchievements.description: Started writing stories at the age of 14 and grew as a novelist with the support of her family.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of 'Sense and Sensibility'
keyAchievements.description: One of her first works published anonymously in 1811.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of 'Pride and Prejudice'
keyAchievements.description: Published in January 1813; the original draft was titled 'First Impressions'.

keyAchievements.title: Publication and Dedication of 'Emma'
keyAchievements.description: Dedicated to the Prince Regent (later George IV) and published in December 1815.

keyAchievements.title: Founder of the 'Great Tradition' of the English Novel
keyAchievements.description: Evaluated as having founded the 'Great Tradition' of the English novel by addressing the lives of upper and middle-class women in 18th-century England with a delicate gaze and witty style.

keyAchievements.title: Socially Critical Realism Literature
keyAchievements.description: Created a sophisticated literary world based on realism, satirizing the material-oriented trends and hypocrisy of contemporary society with keen observation and a sharp critical perspective.

keyAchievements.title: Posthumous Literary Re-evaluation and Pop Culture Influence
keyAchievements.description: Elevated to the literary canon through critics' acclaim from the late 19th century onwards, and in the late 20th century, her works became a popular cult phenomenon, being adapted into films and plays.

faq.question: When was Jane Austen born?
faq.answer: She was born on December 16, 1775, at the rectory in Steventon, Hampshire.

faq.question: What are Jane Austen's representative works?
faq.answer: Her works include 'Sense and Sensibility' (1811), 'Pride and Prejudice' (1813), 'Mansfield Park' (1814), and 'Emma' (1816).

faq.question: What are the characteristics of Jane Austen's novels?
faq.answer: Her novels are characterized by depicting the lives of upper and middle-class women in 18th-century England with a delicate gaze and witty style.

faq.question: Was Jane Austen famous during her lifetime?
faq.answer: She was not very famous during her lifetime, but in the 20th century, her works gained massive popularity and were adapted into films multiple times.

faq.question: Did Jane Austen ever marry?
faq.answer: She had a romance with a young man named Tom Lefroy, but it did not lead to marriage. She received a proposal from Harris Bigg-Wither in 1802 but rejected it the next day.

faq.question: When did Jane Austen die?
faq.answer: She died on July 18, 1817, at the age of 42.

timeline.event: Born in Pentonville, London, England.
Began learning Greek.
Began learning Latin, Euclid, and algebra, and acted as a tutor for his younger siblings.
Reached the level of being able to read original texts of Plato and Demosthenes.
Started studying scholastic logic and original texts of Aristotle's logic.
Began studying political economy.
Stayed in France for a year and took courses in chemistry, zoology, and logic at the Faculty of Sciences in Montpellier.
Suffered from a nervous breakdown.
Founded the Utilitarian Society with his friends.
Published 'A System of Logic'.
Published 'Essays on Some Unsettled Questions of Political Economy'.
Published 'Principles of Political Economy'.
Married Harriet Taylor.
Harriet Taylor died, and he retired from the East India Company.
Published 'On Liberty'.
Published 'Utilitarianism' and 'Considerations on Representative Government'.
Elected as Lord Rector of the University of St Andrews and elected to the House of Commons as a Member of Parliament for the City of Westminster.
Term as Member of Parliament ended.
Became the first person in Parliament to advocate for women's right to vote, and published 'The Subjection of Women'.
Died in Avignon, France, and 'Autobiography' was published.
Published 'Three Essays on Religion'.
Published 'Chapters on Socialism'.

keyAchievements.title: Completion of the System of Logic
keyAchievements.description: Published 'A System of Logic' in 1843, completing Bacon's inductive logic and presenting a groundbreaking methodology for natural science research.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of Liberal Political Theory
keyAchievements.description: Left behind a classic work on the concept of liberty through 'On Liberty', presenting the basic principles of modern liberal democracy by defending freedom of expression as a fundamental right of citizens.

keyAchievements.title: Development of Utilitarianism
keyAchievements.description: Developed Bentham's utilitarianism, his godfather and teacher, into his own system by adding Saint-Simonianism and Romanticism.

keyAchievements.title: Advocacy for Women's Rights and Social Reform
keyAchievements.description: As a Member of Parliament, he advocated for various social reforms including women's rights, proportional representation, labor unions, and cooperative organization of agriculture, and was the first to argue for women's suffrage in Parliament.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneer of Market Socialism Theory
keyAchievements.description: Considered one of the early theorists of market socialism for advocating the replacement of capitalist enterprises with worker cooperatives.

faq.question: How was John Stuart Mill educated?
faq.answer: He was educated primarily by his father, James Mill, and was a precocious child, learning Greek at the age of three and Latin and advanced mathematics at eight.

faq.question: What were Mill's views on religion?
faq.answer: He was critical of religion itself, never having had religious beliefs, denied Christian doctrines, and viewed religion as the greatest enemy of freedom.

faq.question: How did Harriet Taylor influence John Stuart Mill?
faq.answer: Harriet profoundly influenced Mill's thoughts, particularly strengthening his advocacy for women's rights, and influenced the writing of 'On Liberty' and 'The Subjection of Women'.

faq.question: What did John Stuart Mill think about the 'stationary state'?
faq.answer: He believed that a stationary state of capital and wealth might not be terrible but could actually be a much more advanced and happier state than the present, anticipating that humanity would be less concerned with money and focus more on values like dignity, integrity, and justice.

faq.question: Was John Stuart Mill a socialist?
faq.answer: He called himself a socialist and is considered one of the early market socialist theorists for advocating the replacement of capitalist businesses with worker cooperatives.

timeline.event: Born in a rural French village.
Met Jacques Biétrix de Rozières and entered into a long romantic relationship.
In the year her biological father died, she began her career as a public intellectual and wrote the play 'Zamore and Mirza', an anti-slavery work.
Published the pamphlet 'Letters to the People'.
'Zamore and Mirza' was not performed; wrote another play titled 'The Black Market'.
Became a member of the 'Social Club' or 'Society of the Friends of Truth' and began writing the 'Declaration of the Rights of Woman and of the Female Citizen'.
The Jacobins arrested and imprisoned her allies, members of the Girondins.
The Girondin members were executed at the guillotine.
Was sentenced to death by the Jacobins.
Executed.
An intersection in Paris was named 'Olympe de Gouges' in her honor.

keyAchievements.title: Advocacy for Women's Suffrage
keyAchievements.description: Made an innovative argument during the French Revolution that women should also be granted the right to vote.

keyAchievements.title: Author of 'Declaration of the Rights of Woman and of the Female Citizen'
keyAchievements.description: Drafted a declaration articulating the equal rights of women in 1791 out of concern that the French Constitution failed to address women's suffrage.

keyAchievements.title: Anti-Slavery Activism
keyAchievements.description: Advocated for the rights of Black people, writing the anti-slavery play 'Zamore and Mirza' in 1784 and 'The Black Market' in 1789.

keyAchievements.title: Proposals for Marriage Contracts and Protection of Women
keyAchievements.description: Advocated for equal division of wealth between husband and wife, equal property division in case of divorce, and laws to protect impoverished women and their children.

keyAchievements.title: Defense of the Monarchy
keyAchievements.description: Opposed the abolition of the monarchy during the French Revolution and took a stance defending King Louis XVI and Queen Marie Antoinette.

faq.question: Who was Olympe de Gouges?
faq.answer: Olympe de Gouges was an innovative civic activist and a radical advocate for women's rights who argued that women should also be given suffrage during the French Revolution.

faq.question: What is her most famous work?
faq.answer: Her most famous work is the 'Declaration of the Rights of Woman and of the Female Citizen', written in 1791.

faq.question: Why was she executed?
faq.answer: She was executed in 1793 by the Jacobins on charges of inappropriate behavior and attempting to restore the monarchy, citing the contents of her flyer 'The Three Urns, or the Salvation of the Fatherland, by an Aerial Traveler' and her unfinished play 'France Saved, or the Tyrant Dethroned'.

faq.question: What was her stance on the rights of Black people?
faq.answer: She spoke out against black slavery by writing the script for the anti-slavery play 'Zamore and Mirza' in 1784 and writing 'The Black Market' in 1789.

faq.question: What was her stance on the French Revolution?
faq.answer: She welcomed the French Revolution but grew disillusioned that the rights of equality it championed were not extended to women. She opposed the revolutionaries' attempts to abolish the monarchy and supported its preservation.

timeline.event: Henrik Johan Ibsen was born in Skien, Norway.
At age 18, had a son, Hans Jacob Hendrichsen Birkedalen, from a relationship with Else Sophie Jensdatter Birkedalen.
Published his first play, 'Catiline', under the pseudonym 'Brynjolf Bjarme', and had his first stage production, 'The Burial Mound'.
Became the artistic director of the Christiania Theater and married Suzannah Thoresen.
Son Sigurd was born.
Left Christiania for Sorrento, Italy, beginning 27 years of living abroad.
Published the play 'Brand', which brought him critical acclaim and financial success.
Published the play 'Peer Gynt', for which Edvard Grieg composed the incidental music.
Moved from Italy to Dresden, Germany.
Published his only collection of poetry, 'Poems' (Digte).
Wrote 'Emperor and Galilean', dramatizing the life of the Roman Emperor Julian the Apostate, and was knighted.
Moved to Munich and began work on his first modern realistic drama, 'The Pillars of Society'.
His first modern realistic drama, 'The Pillars of Society', was published and first performed.
Published the play 'A Doll's House', which brought him international recognition.
Published the play 'Ghosts', containing commentary on the morality of Ibsen's society.
Published the play 'An Enemy of the People', addressing controversial themes.
Published the play 'The Wild Duck', considered by many to be Ibsen's finest work.
Published the later work 'Hedda Gabler', exploring psychological conflict.
Returned to Christiania, Norway, after 27 years of living abroad.
Published the later work 'The Master Builder', exploring psychological conflict, and was made a Commander.
Received the Grand Cross of the Order of St. Olav.
Suffered several strokes.
Nominated for the Nobel Prize in Literature.
Nominated for the Nobel Prize in Literature.
Nominated for the Nobel Prize in Literature.
Died at his home in Christiania at the age of 78.
The 'Ibsen Year' was celebrated in Norway and many other countries to commemorate the 100th anniversary of his death, and 'A Doll's House' was the world's most performed play.
The 'Delhi Ibsen Festival' began to be held annually in Delhi, India.
Håkon Anton Fagerås created two bronze busts of Ibsen to be installed in Ibsen Park in Sorrento, Italy, and in the Skien municipality.
A marble statue of Ibsen was created for the Ibsen Museum in Oslo.
Google featured a Doodle celebrating Henrik Ibsen's 185th birthday.

keyAchievements.title: Father of Modern Drama
keyAchievements.description: Evaluated as the world's greatest playwright of the 19th century and referred to as the 'Father of Modern Drama'.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneered Realistic Theater
keyAchievements.description: Though he also wrote lyrical epic works, he pioneered realistic theater.

keyAchievements.title: World's Most Performed Playwright
keyAchievements.description: He is the most frequently performed playwright in the world after Shakespeare.

keyAchievements.title: International Recognition
keyAchievements.description: Achieved international recognition with works such as 'Brand', 'Peer Gynt', and 'A Doll's House'.

keyAchievements.title: Redefined the Rules of Drama
keyAchievements.description: Completely rewrote the rules of drama through realism.

keyAchievements.title: Influential Playwright
keyAchievements.description: Influenced figures such as George Bernard Shaw, Oscar Wilde, and James Joyce.

faq.question: When was Henrik Ibsen born and when did he die?
faq.answer: He was born on March 20, 1828, and died on May 23, 1906.

faq.question: How is Henrik Ibsen evaluated as a figure?
faq.answer: He is considered the world's greatest playwright of the 19th century and is known as the 'Father of Modern Drama'.

faq.question: What are Henrik Ibsen's representative works?
faq.answer: His works include 'A Doll's House', 'Ghosts', 'An Enemy of the People', 'The Wild Duck', and 'Peer Gynt'.

faq.question: Did Henrik Ibsen win the Nobel Prize in Literature?
faq.answer: He was nominated for the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1902, 1903, and 1904.

faq.question: What were Henrik Ibsen's political views?
faq.answer: He expressed anarchistic views, stating that the state is a curse upon the individual and should be abolished.

timeline.event: Born into a noble Prussian family in Berlin.
Began his education after his father died.
Educated by a tutor alongside his older brother.
Graduated from the Freiberg School of Mines and became a civil servant in the Prussian Department of Mines.
Toured rock salt mining regions in various Central European countries.
Inherited a large fortune upon his mother's death.
Began his expedition to Spanish America with the support of the Spanish Prime Minister.
Returned from South America.
Published 23 volumes of 'Personal Narrative of Travels to the Equinoctial Regions of the New Continent'.
Published 'Views of Nature' (Ansichten der Natur) in Berlin.
Demonstrated the idea and method of comparing climatic conditions across different countries through the 'Creation of Isothermal Lines'.
Taught physical geography at the University of Vienna.
Died at the age of 90 and was honored with a state funeral.

keyAchievements.title: Authored 'Kosmos'
keyAchievements.description: After observing physical geography and ecology during his expeditions to South America and Central Asia, he compiled vast amounts of data to write 'Kosmos', a masterpiece regarded as a monumental work in modern geography.

keyAchievements.title: Founder of Modern Geography
keyAchievements.description: Became a founder of modern geography along with Carl Ritter.

keyAchievements.title: Naming of the Humboldt Current
keyAchievements.description: The ocean current flowing along the coast of Peru was named the Humboldt Current in honor of his investigations.

keyAchievements.title: Laid the Foundations for Physical Geography and Geophysics
keyAchievements.description: Laid the foundations of physical geography and geophysics through his investigations of the mountain ranges of tropical America.

keyAchievements.title: Observation and Study of Meteor Showers
keyAchievements.description: Conducted observations of meteor showers from Tenerife in the Canary Islands; his research on their periodicity became a foundation for modern astronomical observation.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of 'Views of Nature'
keyAchievements.description: Investigated and studied Mount Vesuvius in Italy and published 'Views of Nature' in Berlin in 1807.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of Isothermal Lines
keyAchievements.description: Demonstrated the method of comparing climatic conditions of various countries in 'Des Lignes Isothermes' (1817) and discovered the rate of temperature decrease with increasing altitude.

keyAchievements.title: Research on Earth's Magnetism
keyAchievements.description: Discovered that the strength of the Earth's magnetic field decreases from the poles toward the equator.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of International Magnetic Observatories and Meteorological Institute
keyAchievements.description: Created an international network of magnetic observatories and established a meteorological institute in Prussia.

keyAchievements.title: Extensive Specimen Collection and Research
keyAchievements.description: Investigated the relationship between plants and their environment, collecting a massive herbarium of 60,000 specimens, which included thousands of new species and genera.

faq.question: Who was Alexander von Humboldt?
faq.answer: He was a German geographer, natural scientist, naturalist, and explorer, who along with Carl Ritter, became a founder of modern geography.

faq.question: What is his representative work?
faq.answer: His magnum opus is 'Kosmos', based on data from his expeditions in South America and Central Asia, which is regarded as a monumental achievement in modern geography.

faq.question: How is the Humboldt Current related to him?
faq.answer: The Humboldt Current was named in his honor to commemorate his investigation of the ocean current flowing along the coast of Peru.

faq.question: In what fields did he leave a legacy?
faq.answer: He made tremendous contributions to a variety of fields including flora and fauna, astronomy, climate, mineralogy, and oceanography, in addition to geography, natural science, natural history, and exploration.
